message:
refactor(fcp-cbor): simplify canonicalize_map capped-writer error mapping by threading byte_limit as a parameter

Cleanup on the `fix(fcp-cbor): enforce per-key byte budget in
canonicalize_map` commit (d207e599). The first cut of that fix
carried the byte limit through a custom `PayloadTooLargeContext`
payload attached to the `io::Error` inner value, and then downcast
it out of the error on the way back up:

    fn map_cbor_serialize_error(err: ciborium::ser::Error<std::io::Error>)
        -> SerializationError
    {
        match err.into_inner() {
            Some(inner) if inner.kind() == io::ErrorKind::OutOfMemory => {
                SerializationError::PayloadTooLarge {
                    len: usize::MAX,
                    max: inner
                        .get_ref()
                        .and_then(|payload| payload
                            .downcast_ref::<PayloadTooLargeContext>())
                        .map_or(MAX_CANONICAL_OBJECT_BYTES, |ctx| ctx.max),
                }
            }
            Some(inner) => SerializationError::CborSerialize(
                ciborium::ser::Error::Io(inner)),
            None => SerializationError::CborSerialize(
                ciborium::ser::Error::Value(format!("{err}"))),
        }
    }

The `Any::downcast_ref` hop depended on io::Error's inner-value
preservation (which is documented but has corner cases around
reborrowing) and used the MAX_CANONICAL_OBJECT_BYTES as a fallback
when the downcast failed, which could report the wrong `max` to
the caller if the enforcement was done at a site with a different
byte_limit (e.g., a future caller using canonicalize_map_with_
limit with a smaller cap).

This commit replaces the downcast-based plumbing with a simpler
shape: the byte_limit is passed in as an explicit parameter
everywhere it's needed.

- `measured_cbor_len_with_limit(value, max_len, byte_limit)` —
  takes the byte_limit explicitly and forwards it to
  `map_capped_writer_error` when a serialize error bubbles out.
- `append_canonical_map_key_bytes(key, scratch, byte_limit)` —
  uses the same `map_capped_writer_error(err, byte_limit)` path
  for the `into_writer(key, &mut writer)` call.
- New `map_capped_writer_error(err, byte_limit) -> SerializationError`:

      fn map_capped_writer_error(
          err: ciborium::ser::Error<std::io::Error>,
          byte_limit: usize,
      ) -> SerializationError {
          match &err {
              ciborium::ser::Error::Io(inner)
                  if inner.kind() == io::ErrorKind::OutOfMemory =>
              {
                  SerializationError::PayloadTooLarge {
                      len: byte_limit.saturating_add(1),
                      max: byte_limit,
                  }
              }
              _ => SerializationError::CborSerialize(err),
          }
      }

  Reports `len: byte_limit + 1` (we know it exceeds the limit but
  not by how much, at the point where the writer's OutOfMemory
  fires) and the exact `byte_limit` that was in force. No downcast.

- `PayloadTooLargeContext` is removed entirely.
- `payload_too_large_io_error(max)` now constructs its io::Error
  with a plain formatted message string instead of the old
  `PayloadTooLargeContext` payload:

      fn payload_too_large_io_error(max: usize) -> io::Error {
          io::Error::new(
              io::ErrorKind::OutOfMemory,
              format!("payload exceeds {max} bytes"),
          )
      }

No behavior change for callers; `SerializationError::PayloadTooLarge
{ len, max }` still fires with the `max` matching whatever the
caller passed as the byte_limit. The `len` field gains slightly
tighter semantics (`byte_limit + 1` instead of `usize::MAX`),
which is more useful for logs.

Net LOC delta: -4 insertions, -26 deletions on the error-mapping
machinery.
